summary: This brief post highlights the underutilized Command Window feature in Visual Studio .NET 2003, noting that many developers are unaware of its command alias shortcuts and the distinction between immediate and command modes. Atwood shares his use of the '?' command for dumping structure contents while acknowledging it as a basic approach. He looks forward to custom data visualizers coming in Visual Studio Whidbey (2005), which will provide better visual representations of data structures. For those who can't wait, he points to existing third-party visualizer tools available for VS.NET 2003.
key_points: The Command Window in VS.NET 2003 has many underused features including command alias shortcuts
Switching between immediate mode and command mode is done with '>cmd' and 'immed' commands
Visual Studio Whidbey will introduce custom data visualizers for better debugging
Third-party visualizers like DataSet visualizer and VSTweak hacks are available now for VS.NET 2003
money_quotes: One of the most underappreciated features of Visual Studio .NET 2003 is the Command Window.
This works, but it's kinda ghetto.
And naturally you can extend the visualizers with custom viewers for whatever crazy data structure you're creating, too.
key_insight: Effective debugging often means discovering and mastering the underutilized features already built into your development tools.
